full_response:
Step 1.  Choose coordinates adapted to constant-proper-acceleration motion  
The world-line of any uniformly accelerated observer can be written, in units with c = 1,

  t(τ) = χ sinh η , x(τ) = χ cosh η                     (1)

where  
• χ = constant labels a particular observer,  
• η is a common “Rindler time’’ coordinate,  
• τ is the proper time along that observer’s world-line.

The Minkowski line-element in these Rindler coordinates is  

  ds² = –χ² dη² + dχ² + dy² + dz².                     (2)

Step 2.  Relate χ to proper acceleration  
For an observer that sits at fixed χ, the 4-acceleration magnitude is

  α = 1/χ.                     (3)

Hence χ is simply the inverse of the proper acceleration.

Step 3.  Assign χ to the two ends of the rocket  
Top of the rocket (where “you’’ sit):  
 α_top = 1 g ≃ 1 ly yr⁻² ⇒ α_top = 1 yr⁻¹ (because c = 1 ly yr⁻¹).  
 χ_top = 1/α_top = 1 yr = 1 ly.                     (4)

The rocket is Born-rigid and has proper length  
 L = ½ ly.  

At any instant of common Rindler time η the proper distance between the two ends is just Δχ = L, so  

 χ_bottom = χ_top + L = 1 ly + 0.5 ly = 1.5 ly.       (5)

Step 4.  Express proper-time intervals in terms of dη  
For a world-line at fixed χ, equation (2) with dχ = 0 gives

  dτ = χ dη.                     (6)

Hence for simultaneous events (same dη)

  dτ_top = χ_top dη, dτ_bottom = χ_bottom dη

⇒ dτ_top / dτ_bottom = χ_top / χ_bottom = 1 ly / 1.5 ly = 2/3.   (7)

Step 5.  Apply the ratio to one second measured at the bottom  
Let the clock at the bottom advance by Δτ_bottom = 1 s.  
Then

  Δτ_top = (2/3) Δτ_bottom = 2/3 s ≃ 0.67 s.                     (8)

So your wrist-watch at the top ticks more slowly by the factor 2/3.

\boxed{\displaystyle \Delta\tau_{\text{top}} \approx 0.67 \text{ s for every } 1 \text{ s at the bottom}}  
Confidence: 93%
